sourceMovie.title: House
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: House (1977) stuck because it commits to its comedy premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Nobuhiko Obayashi's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The comedy and fantasy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Beetlejuice Beetlejuice
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If House clicked for you, Beetlejuice Beetlejuice (2024) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy and fantasy, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If House clicked for you, The House (2022) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy and fantasy, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after House?
faq.answer: Try Beetlejuice Beetlejuice, The House, Beetlejuice—each shares DNA with House while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is House?
faq.answer: House (1977) is best described as Comedy, Fantasy, Horror. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is House worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this comedy lane.
